preamble: The shift from the bilabial nasal [m] to a glottal stop [ʔ] is a form of debuccalization. It typically occurs in syllable-final (coda) positions when a language simplifies its phonology toward an open-syllable structure. The oral closure (lips) is lost, leaving only the glottal tension that originally accompanied the consonant.

examples.note: Through a process of vowel apocope and subsequent debuccalization, final nasals often surface as glottal stops in rapid speech.
Historical nasal codas have merged into nasalized vowels followed by a glottal stop.
